description: Hand-labelled eval set: human transcripts of hard pages (degraded scans, handwriting) that carry no born-digital ground truth. Copy this file to labelset.json, then fill in the matching .txt transcripts. Page indices are 0-BASED: use `page` for one page, or `pages` for a document that spans several (the transcript then covers the whole span, in reading order). `pdf` resolves from the dir you run the eval in; `transcript` resolves beside this manifest. Optional `render: true` renders a BORN-DIGITAL page to an image-only PDF (drops the text layer) so the pipeline OCRs it — turning a known-text multi-column page into a scan with a 100%-certain reading order (seed the transcript from the page's exact text, then certify the order). See Docs/eval-labelling.md.

labels.note: BORN-DIGITAL two-column prose rendered to a scan (render:true) — a multi-column reading-order oracle with exact, certain text. Seed the transcript from the page's text layer, then certify left-column-then-right reading order against the rendered image. Reading order shows up as a high recall with a higher CER.
The headline value case: 2-page handwritten Mandelson->Lammy letter. Source text layer ~3 chars; tesseract recovers almost nothing.
Printed cover email captured as a scan (no text layer) — a printed-text-on-scan recognition floor.
Degraded Thai scan — exercises the Thai recogniser + Typhoon reader. Transcribe in Thai (Unicode, NFC).
Rotated page — exercises rotation-aware reading order. Verify which page is the rotated one and adjust `page` if needed.

Redacted email (1 page) — MIXED content: it carries a partial text layer AND gets OCR'd. The exact text layer beats the OCR (it already covers the page), so no overlay is added and search hits aren't doubled; the scorecard shows searchability `via txt` (the source text layer), not an OCR overlay. Mark blacked-out spans as [REDACTED] in the transcript so recall isn't penalised for unreadable ink.
